reviews.summary: This paper asks whether early ranking targets for fixed-budget compositional text-to-image continuation should be decomposed into atomic constraints (count, attribute-binding, relation) or collapsed into a scalar target. Using frozen Stable Diffusion v1.5, GenEval-style prompts, and matched lightweight MLP probes, the authors find that decomposition does not yield a statistically reliable improvement (p=0.6033) and a simpler best-of-2 full-completion baseline outperforms both approaches. The paper explicitly presents this as a negative result under a local surrogate evaluator.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Extensive search confirms the paper does not plagiarize or duplicate existing work, but the contribution is extremely narrow. Guo 2026 (arXiv:2603.02829, Probe-Select) already demonstrates early-trajectory quality prediction for generate-then-select diffusion pipelines. Cui 2026 (arXiv:2602.23783, Diffusion Probe) uses CNN probes on early cross-attention maps for quality forecasting. Yu 2025 (arXiv:2505.13740, CompLift, ICML 2025) decomposes compositional prompts into sub-conditions using lift scores. Jaiswal 2026 (arXiv:2601.15286) uses iterative refinement with VLM critics for compositional generation. Ma 2025 (CVPR 2025) provides the broader inference-time scaling framework. The paper's contribution—testing decomposed vs. scalar targets for early ranking—sits at the intersection of these existing ideas but adds only a controlled negative result on a single small-scale configuration. The negative finding, while honest, is not surprising given the weak probe quality (ROC-AUC ~0.70) and the narrow budget/feature setup.
strengths: Exceptional honesty and scientific integrity: the paper clearly states its negative result, explicitly acknowledges that official GenEval was not used, documents evaluator-benchmark disagreement (78.8% prompt agreement), and lists all deviations from the original plan.

Very high clarity and organization: the writing is precise, claims are carefully scoped, and the paper consistently distinguishes between local heuristic evaluation and official benchmark performance.

Strong reproducibility package: complete experiment code exists for all 6 methods, seeds are fixed and documented, hyperparameters are fully specified, hardware is recorded, and an artifact-to-claim map (Table 5) traces every paper number to its source file.
Appropriate statistical methodology: bootstrap confidence intervals, paired permutation tests, and proper reporting of p-values rather than cherry-picked point estimates.
All 14 references are verified as real publications with correct metadata—no fabricated citations.
weaknesses: The contribution is too narrow and the negative result too unsurprising to meet the acceptance threshold. The probe ROC-AUC is only 0.70, the feature set excludes cross-attention (acknowledged as unimplemented), and the budget is tiny (B40). A negative result under these weak conditions tells us little about whether decomposition would help with better probes, richer features, or larger budgets.

The evaluation uses a local heuristic surrogate with only 78.8% agreement with official GenEval at the prompt level and 0% counting success locally vs. 34.9% officially. This severely limits the external validity of any conclusion drawn.

The experimental scale is very small: 80 test prompts, 3 seeds, one generator (SD v1.5), one budget configuration. The confidence intervals are correspondingly wide, making it impossible to draw strong conclusions in either direction.

The best-of-2 full-completion baseline (0.1917) outperforms all early-ranking methods, suggesting the prefix-based approach itself is not competitive, which undermines the relevance of comparing decomposed vs. scalar targets within it.
Several planned experiments were not completed (official GenEval evaluation, human-label sensitivity, cross-attention features), leaving the paper as an incomplete execution of its own research plan.

The ablation showing that removing preview features hurts more than changing the target structure (Table 2) further undermines the paper's central question—the decomposition axis appears to be the wrong lever.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper is commendably honest but the contribution does not meet the bar for a top venue. The central finding—that decomposed early targets do not reliably outperform scalar targets under a weak probe and local surrogate evaluator—is neither surprising nor actionable. To strengthen the work, the authors should: (1) implement the missing cross-attention features, which prior work (Cui 2026) shows are the most predictive early signals; (2) run the official GenEval evaluation pipeline to establish credible benchmark numbers; (3) scale to larger budgets (B80, B160) to test whether decomposition becomes useful when the probe has more signal; (4) use a stronger base model (SDXL or a DiT-based model) to increase baseline compositional success rates from the current ~15-19% range; (5) complete the human-label sensitivity analysis. The paper would also benefit from connecting more explicitly to the inference-time scaling literature (Ma 2025), since the B40 prune-continue schedule is essentially a constrained version of that framework. As it stands, the paper reads as a well-documented pilot study rather than a complete research contribution.
questions_for_authors: Given that the local evaluator shows 0% counting success while official GenEval shows 34.9%, how confident are you that the ranking behavior of the decomposed probe would be the same under official evaluation?
Have you tried increasing the prefix length beyond 4 steps? The probe ROC-AUC of 0.70 suggests the 4-step prefix may simply not contain enough information for meaningful discrimination.
The ablation shows preview features matter more than decomposition. Would a probe trained on CLIP features alone (without latent statistics) perform comparably or better?
Why was cross-attention extraction not implemented despite being in the original proposal? This seems like the most natural feature for compositional constraint prediction.
Would you expect different conclusions with a budget of B80 or B160, where the probe has more candidates to discriminate among?
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity is sound. All numbers in Tables 1-2 match results.json within rounding precision. The bootstrap CI [-0.0167, 0.0417] and p-value 0.6033 match exactly. Experiment source code exists for all 6 methods (exp/*/run.py) plus shared infrastructure (exp/shared/core.py). Probe training histories in results.json show plausible learning curves (19 epochs for decomposed, 12 for shared-head, 6 for no-preview). Figures exist as both PNG and PDF. The paper honestly flags that the manual-label sensitivity experiment was skipped and that official GenEval was not run for the main comparison. No integrity concerns.

reviews.summary: The paper studies a narrowly scoped question for compositional text-to-image generation: under a fixed B40 prune-continue budget and a local OWLv2+CLIP surrogate evaluator, whether ranking partial diffusion trajectories with decomposed atomic constraints is better than ranking them with a scalar early target. Using SD-v1.5, deterministic GenEval-style prompts, matched lightweight probes, and three test seeds, the paper finds a small but statistically insignificant advantage for decomposition over the scalar baseline and worse performance than a simple best-of-2 full-completion baseline. The paper is explicit that this is a negative result under a local surrogate rather than official held-out GenEval.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed more than the required five distinct searches. Exact-title searches on Google Scholar and Semantic Scholar returned no results, suggesting the manuscript itself is not previously published. However, core-technique searches surfaced very close prior scaffolding: early outcome prediction/selective continuation for diffusion trajectories in 'Toward Early Quality Assessment of Text-to-Image Diffusion Models' and 'Diffusion Probe: Generated Image Result Prediction Using CNN Probes'; broader inference-time search in 'Inference-Time Scaling for Diffusion Models beyond Scaling Denoising Steps' (https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.09732); compositional condition scoring/reranking in 'Improving Compositional Generation with Diffusion Models Using Lift Scores' (https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.13740); and compositional guidance/improved faithfulness work such as Composable Diffusion (https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.01714), Attend-and-Excite (https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.13826), Fine-Grained Alignment and Noise Refinement (https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.06506), Iterative Refinement (https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.15286), and TokenCompose/CVPR 2024 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.03626). Searches of recent venue papers also showed that token/constraint-level compositional supervision is already an active line. My conclusion is that the paper's contribution is an incremental combination of existing ideas: early diffusion ranking plus compositional atomic scoring. I did not find a substantially identical paper with the same exact setup, but the novelty is limited and clearly below clear-accept level.
strengths: The paper is unusually honest about its claim boundary: it explicitly states that the result is negative and only supports conclusions under a local surrogate evaluator, not official GenEval.

The experimental comparison is reasonably controlled: generator, prefix length, budget, and feature vector are fixed while decomposed vs scalar targets are compared with closely matched probe architectures.

Artifact provenance is relatively good. The main table values match recomputation from exp/main_experiment/trials.jsonl, the runtime numbers match per-method results.json, the bootstrap intervals match the saved raw trials, and the Python sources compile.
The workspace contains substantial supporting material: source code, per-method trial logs, manifests, supervision artifacts, figures, and reproducibility notes.
The paper includes useful ablations and diagnostics, including shared-head and no-preview variants, calibration plots, and an oracle hit-rate diagnostic.
weaknesses: Novelty is weak. The paper sits very close to existing early-quality prediction/selective continuation work and existing compositional condition-scoring work; the main contribution is a narrow recombination rather than a clearly new method.

The central empirical limitation is evaluation credibility. The headline comparison is not on official held-out GenEval, but on a local OWLv2+CLIP heuristic whose own appendix shows substantial mismatch with official-style labels, especially for counting.

Because the evaluator is local and imperfect, it is difficult to interpret the scientific significance of the negative result: the paper may be measuring properties of the surrogate more than properties of compositional early ranking.
The strongest baseline, best-of-2 full completion reranked by CLIP similarity, outperforms the proposed decomposed method, which further weakens the practical significance of the contribution.

Experimental rigor is only moderate: there are three test seeds and bootstrap intervals for the main contrast, but no official held-out benchmark evaluation, no completed human sensitivity study, and no stronger test of cross-evaluator robustness.
The claimed parser audit is hard to treat as strong evidence because the saved audit file is a canned JSON artifact rather than an independently documented review process.
The implemented feature set omits the cross-attention features mentioned in the original proposal; while the paper discloses this, it reduces the distinctiveness of the final method.
reviews.detailed_feedback: To make this publishable, the paper needs a stronger scientific hook than the current local-surrogate negative comparison. The most important revision would be to rerun the headline method comparison on official held-out GenEval, or on a benchmark-faithful reproduction with a convincing validation against the official stack, so that the conclusion reflects compositional generation rather than surrogate-label behavior. The novelty claim should be further downgraded and positioned explicitly as a benchmark-specialized extension of early diffusion ranking rather than a new inference-time method. If the authors want to retain the decomposed-target angle, they need a sharper differentiator, such as a principled aggregation rule, a budget-aware decision policy, or richer early features that are uniquely useful for atomic constraints. I would also strengthen the paper by showing cross-evaluator robustness, adding confidence intervals for all headline methods, and documenting the parser audit/manual sensitivity procedure in a way that is genuinely auditable rather than template-like. As it stands, the paper is careful and reasonably reproducible, but the combination of low novelty and evaluator-dependent evidence keeps it below threshold.
questions_for_authors: Can the full held-out comparison be run with official GenEval rather than the local OWLv2+CLIP heuristic, and if not, why not?
How sensitive are the conclusions to the surrogate evaluator design choices, especially the counting threshold and single-crop color decision rule?
Why does the decomposed target fail to beat best-of-2 full completion under the same B40 budget despite sometimes improving oracle-hit diagnostics?
Can you provide a stronger justification that the saved parser audit reflects actual independent manual review rather than a post hoc template artifact?
Did you test any richer early features tailored to atomic constraints, e.g. cross-attention or grounded token-level signals, and if so what happened?
reviews.integrity_check: Results appear mostly genuine. The paper's main table values match recomputation from exp/main_experiment/trials.jsonl, the per-method runtime numbers match exp/*/results.json, the bootstrap intervals and reported p-value are reproducible from the saved trials with the script's permutation settings, source code is present and compiles, and event logs/manifests point to concrete generated images. I did not rerun the full image-generation pipeline, so this is a sanity check rather than a full reproduction. The main residual integrity concern is not fabrication but evaluator validity: the study relies on a local surrogate, the manual sensitivity study was omitted, and the parser audit evidence is weaker than ideal.

reviews.summary: This paper investigates whether decomposing early ranking targets into atomic constraints (count, attribute-binding, relation) improves over a scalar target for fixed-budget compositional text-to-image generation. Using Stable Diffusion v1.5 with a B40 compute budget (6 prefixes at 4 steps, continue 1 for 16 steps), the authors find a negative result: the decomposed target achieves 0.1625 prompt-level all_correct versus 0.1500 for the scalar target, but the 95% bootstrap CI [-0.0167, 0.0417] includes zero and the permutation test gives p=0.6033.
reviews.novelty_assessment: My search revealed several closely related works that diminish the novelty claim: (1) Guo et al. (2026) 'Toward Early Quality Assessment of Text-to-Image Diffusion Models' (arXiv:2603.02829) introduces Probe-Select, which predicts final quality from early denoiser activations at 20% of trajectory, achieving >60% cost reduction—this directly overlaps with the early assessment methodology. (2) Liu et al. (2022) 'Compositional Visual Generation with Composable Diffusion Models' (ECCV) and related works on composable diffusion already explore decompositional approaches for generation. (3) Recent works like VisionReward (2024) and DecompDPO explicitly compare scalar vs. decomposed preference modeling. The specific combination of early prefix assessment with compositional constraint decomposition is new, but the negative result and narrow scope limit the contribution. The paper is essentially a controlled ablation study rather than a novel methodological contribution.
strengths: The paper is refreshingly honest about its negative result and does not overclaim. The authors appropriately scope their conclusions to 'this implementation does not provide reliable evidence that it helps.'

Experimental design is well-controlled: matched probe architectures (shared trunk [256,128]), same prefix features, same budget, deterministic prompt decomposition with manual audit (30/30 exact matches).
Thorough statistical reporting: prompt-level bootstrap CIs, paired permutation tests, multiple seeds (11, 17, 23), and clear distinction between point estimates and statistical significance.
Reproducibility artifacts are well-documented: split construction, hyperparameters, hardware specs, saved feature vectors, and explicit artifact-to-claim mapping in Appendix.
The ablation study showing preview features matter more than target decomposition (0.0208 vs 0.0125 delta) provides a useful practical insight.
weaknesses: The primary limitation is evaluation credibility: official GenEval was not executed. All headline numbers use a local OWLv2-plus-CLIP heuristic evaluator that shows only 78.8% prompt-level agreement with official GenEval on train+val, with particularly weak agreement (65.1%) on counting prompts. The paper is therefore about a surrogate metric, not established benchmark performance.

The scope is extremely narrow: one generator (SD v1.5), one budget (B40), one deterministic prompt subset (GenEval templates), one local evaluator. The result should not be generalized beyond this specific configuration.

The best-of-2 full-completion baseline (0.1917) substantially outperforms both early-target methods, suggesting the early-stopping approach itself may be the wrong research direction rather than the target decomposition.
Cross-attention statistics mentioned in the proposal were not implemented, and the human-label sensitivity experiment was skipped—acknowledged deviations that weaken the study.

The contribution is primarily a negative result on a narrow design question. While negative results can be valuable, NeurIPS typically expects stronger scientific advances or insights that open new research directions.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper is well-written and methodologically sound for its scope, but faces a fundamental challenge: it presents a negative result on a narrow engineering question using a non-standard evaluator. To strengthen this work, I suggest: (1) Run official GenEval evaluation—the current local heuristic evaluation undermines all quantitative claims. The 21.2% disagreement rate with official labels on prompt-level correctness (especially 34.9% on counting) means the reported statistics may not reflect actual compositional generation performance. (2) Expand the analysis to explain WHY decomposition doesn't help—the probe achieves ROC-AUC 0.738 on validation, yet this doesn't translate to downstream gains. Is it a capacity issue? Feature quality? The calibration curves look reasonable but the end-to-end gain is absent. (3) Consider whether the B40 budget is too small for meaningful differentiation—at only 4 prefix steps, the signal may be too noisy. (4) The best-of-2 baseline being stronger suggests the paper should pivot to analyzing when (if ever) early stopping makes sense, rather than focusing on target decomposition. (5) If resubmitted, broaden the contribution by providing theoretical or empirical analysis of when decomposed supervision helps versus when it suffers from 'advantage cancellation' (as discussed in D3PO and related MORL literature).
questions_for_authors: Why was official GenEval not executed? The paper mentions MMDetection stack unavailability—is this a temporary limitation or fundamental? Would running official GenEval change your conclusions?

The decomposed probe achieves validation ROC-AUC 0.738 but doesn't improve downstream selection. What explains this disconnect between probe quality and selection quality? Is the probe learning spurious correlations?

Given that best-of-2 full completion substantially outperforms both early-target methods (0.1917 vs 0.1625), should the conclusion be that early stopping at 4 steps is fundamentally limited, rather than that decomposition doesn't help?
How sensitive are results to the prefix length τ=4? Would a longer prefix (e.g., τ=8 or 10) provide stronger signal for decomposition to show benefits?
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity check: PASSED. I verified that (1) all main result numbers in Table 1 match results.json (e.g., decomposed 0.1625, scalar 0.1500, best-of-2 0.1917), (2) bootstrap CI [-0.0167, 0.0417] and p-value 0.6033 match the JSON, (3) experiment source code exists in exp/shared/core.py and exp/main_experiment/run.py implementing the described method, (4) probe training logs show actual epochs with validation AUC progression, (5) references checked against Semantic Scholar/Google Scholar are real papers with correct titles/venues (e.g., Liu et al. 2022 ECCV, Park et al. 2021 NeurIPS D&B, Guo et al. 2026 arXiv). Note: Some citations have future dates (2026) which is unconventional but the papers exist on arXiv. No evidence of fabricated results or hallucinated citations.

The most important revision would be to rerun the headline method comparison on official held-out GenEval, or on a benchmark-faithful reproduction with a convincing validation against the official stack, so that the conclusion reflects compositional generation rather than surrogate-label behavior. The paper's main table values match recomputation from exp/main_experiment/trials.jsonl, the per-method runtime numbers match exp/*/results.json, the bootstrap intervals and reported p-value are reproducible from the saved trials with the script's permutation settings, source code is present and compiles, and event logs/manifests point to concrete generated images.
